sanskrit_devanagari:
तस्मात्त्वमिन्द्रियाण्यादौ नियम्य भरतर्षभ ।
पाप्मानं प्रजहि ह्येनं ज्ञानविज्ञाननाशनम् ॥ ४१ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
tasmāt tvam indriyāṇy ādau
niyamya bharatarṣabha |
pāpmānaṁ prajahi hy enaṁ
jñāna-vijñāna-nāśanam || 41 ||

anvaya.meaning: O chief amongst the descendants of Bharata
and scientific knowledge of the pure soul

translations.verbatim_quote: Therefore, O Arjuna, best of the Bhāratas, in the very beginning curb this great symbol of sin [lust] by regulating the senses, and slay this destroyer of knowledge and self-realization.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Lord advised Arjuna to regulate the senses from the very beginning so that he could curb the greatest sinful enemy, lust, which destroys the urge for self-realization and specific knowledge of the self.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.41 Since this is so, therefore, O scion of the Bharata dynasty, adau niyamya, after first controlling; indriyani, the organs; prajahihi, renounce; enam, this one, the enemy under consideration; which is papmanam, sinful-which is desire that is accustomed to sinning; and jnana-vi
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: tasmāt — therefore — tvam — you — indriyāṇi ādau niyamya — controlling the senses first — bharatarṣabha — O bull of the Bharatas — pāpmānaṁ prajahi hi enam — kill this evil one — jñāna-vijñāna-nāśanam — destroyer of jñāna (knowledge of the Self) and vijñāna (the specialised lived realisation that follows). The verse names the order: senses first because they are the entry, then the higher layers per 3.42.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.41 For whatever reason a person engaged in Jnana Yoga, which is of the nature of abandoning the activities of all the senses, should control this enemy in the shape of desire which turns him away from the self through creating infatuation for objects of the senses — for the same
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: the first move is to bring the senses under restraint — they are the layer at which kāma first enters. Then the deeper kill must address kāma's seat in the higher layers (mind and intellect, named in 3.42). The verse opens the layered prescription that closes at 3.43.

positions.rendering: therefore, O Arjuna, best of the Bhāratas, in the very beginning curb this great symbol of sin [lust] by regulating the senses, and slay this destroyer of knowledge and self-realization
therefore, O best of the Bharatas, in the very beginning bring the senses under control and slay this enemy called desire, which is the destroyer of knowledge and realization

indriyāṇi ādau niyamya — the senses controlled first, because they are kāma's entry point; pāpmānaṁ prajahi enam — kill this evil one; jñāna-vijñāna-nāśanam — the destroyer of jñāna (knowledge of the truth) and vijñāna (specialised, lived knowledge that follows realisation)

the first action is to bring the senses under restraint, since they are the layer at which kāma initially enters; then the deeper kill must address its seat in mind and intellect; the verse begins the multi-layer prescription that 3.42-3.43 completes

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both commentators read ādau (first) as ordinal — the senses are the FIRST layer to address, but not the only one. The verse begins the prescription that 3.42-3.43 will complete by naming the higher layers. Translators capture the surface but sometimes lose the 'first, then more' structure.

literal_meaning: Therefore, you, O bull of the Bharatas, controlling the senses first, kill this destroyer of jñāna and vijñāna.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna gives the first prescription. tasmāt (therefore — following from 3.40's diagnosis) — control the senses first (indriyāṇy ādau niyamya). Then kill this enemy (pāpmānaṁ prajahi enam) — the destroyer of jñāna (knowledge) and vijñāna (specialised knowledge / discrimination). The verse establishes the order: senses first because they are the entry point, but the goal is killing the enemy, not just controlling its entry.
